nodes.text: The drive home is fifteen minutes.
He pulls into the driveway at eleven fifty-two.

His mother's car is in the driveway. The garage is open. His father's truck is on the street, where Bill parks on Fridays because the trash trucks come Friday afternoons and Bill has been parking on the street for the trash trucks since 2014.
He goes in through the garage.
[shot:closeup bill]Bill is at the kitchen table with the Sentinel.

Bill is, by Bill tradition, doing the Friday crossword in pen. His mother is, by the angle of the bedroom door from the hallway, in the bedroom — the night-shift sleep, the eight-AM-to-three-PM sleep she does on days she works a ten-hour straight night. Daisy, the family's twelve-year-old golden, is at Bill's feet.
Light. They're easing us off for tomorrow.
[shot:closeup bill]Bill, by the small Bill-tradition he has been doing all summer when the conversation about football has had to happen, looks up from the crossword.
I think so. Coach K hasn't said. But I think so. The half-line work this week was good. The cone work is where he wants it. I think I'm at TE-1.
If you are. Tell Coach Dale to put you in the rotation for the second-half conditioning. The first-half guys get tired. Second-half guys get the points.
Coach Dale doesn't do that, Dad. Coach K does.
Then tell Coach K. Same advice.
I'm not telling you to coach yourself, Ben. I'm telling you what I know. Use it or don't.
[shot:establish]Ben, at the kitchen counter, makes himself a sandwich.

He eats the sandwich. He drinks two glasses of water. He sits with Daisy on the couch for twenty minutes. He watches, with the sound off, the noon news, which has — at twelve oh-five — a story about the heat that has been in every noon broadcast for three weeks and that says, today, the heat is going to break by Tuesday.
I'm going back. Chart goes up at noon.
I don't know. For — for the advice.
Ben. The advice is free. The thanks is unnecessary.
[shot:insert crossword]He looks down at the crossword.
[shot:closeup ben~]In the Civic he sits for a count with both hands on the wheel.

He has, in the past two hours, eaten a sandwich, sat with the dog, watched five minutes of the noon news, and had a forty-second exchange with his father at the kitchen table that was, by Bill's standard, one of the longest forty-second exchanges Bill has had with him this summer.
He drives back to the school.
The chart goes up at noon.
